of firms. Consequently, MPK resembles the endogenous state variable
that determines investment for all firms, and low MPK directly implies
low investment.
The inability of the no-jump model to produce large movements in
sales and MPK, as observed in the data, motivates our next estimation.
This approach targets both the general moments in Panel A and the
jump-related moments in Panel B of Table 6. As a result, the estimation
yields a significantly higher value for\sigmaand a lower value forc, as
shown in Table 7.
This outcome arises because increasing Gaussian volatility while
reducing adjustment costs can generate occasional jumps and a higher
dispersion in sales growth. However, the overall jump probability
remains extremely low at just0 . 0 0 2 \%, far from the data value of
1 . 6 2 \%. Similar to the case where jumps are not explicitly targeted,
the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio remains nearly empty, and the observed
jumps in Panel B stem solely from large random realizations of Gaussian
shocks.
4.2.2. Model implications without ‘‘investing in misallocation’’
To address the second question posed in Section 4.2, we conduct a
counterfactual analysis using our baseline model. In this counterfactual
scenario, jumps in productivity are still present in the data-generating
process, but firms’ investment policies ignore the potential of future
jumps. That is, high-type firms do not engage in ‘‘investing in mis-
allocation’’, meaning they do not invest more than a low-type firm
with the same productivity and capital, even though they have a
positive probability of experiencing a jump and additional capital could
potentially increase their likelihood of jumping. The only distinction
between low-type and high-type firms in this counterfactual scenario
arises from the realized jumps, which are not anticipated. For this
counterfactual exercise, we maintain the same model parameters as in
the baseline model.
Table 8 presents a comparison of the simulated moments in the
baseline model and the counterfactual model. The results show that
the counterfactual model generates significantly lower investment dis-
persion for young firms compared to the baseline model and the
data (Panel A), indicating that the difference in investment dispersion
between young and mature firms can be attributed to high-type firms’
heightened investment in anticipation of jumps. A reduction in sales
growth dispersion is also observed, although not to the same extent
as the decline in young firms’ investment dispersion. Despite these dif-
ferences, the counterfactual model simulations still exhibit jumps, and
their magnitudes closely resemble those observed in the data, albeit the
jump probability for young firms drops from2 . 3 \%in the baseline model
to1 . 1 \%in the counterfactual (Panel B). Similarly, the unconditional
probability of jumps is significantly lower, at0 . 6 2 \%compared to1 . 3 2 \%
in the baseline model. This reduction arises from the jump specification
in Eq. (6). In the counterfactual scenario, where firms ignore potential
productivity jumps, they also overlook the heightened jump probability
associated with capital accumulation. Consequently, firms do not invest
to increase their jump probability, leading to an overall decline in the
average jump probability.
Panel C of Table 4 illustrates that in the counterfactual model, the
joint distribution of investment and MPK leads to the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )
portfolio share becoming zero. When firms ignore jumps in their in-
vestment policies, investment is solely determined by the current MPK,
thereby eliminating the occurrence of high investment despite a low
MPK. Consequently, our model predicts that this portfolio, which con-
stitutes2 1 \%of Compustat firms, would be absent when the mechanism
in our model concerning the impact of anticipated jumps on investment
is shut down, even though jump realizations still form part of the
data-generating process.
We also examine the relationship between predicted jumps, in-
vestment, and MPK in both the baseline and alternative models. The
results of this comparison can be found in Table 9. In the data, we
observe that investment positively predicts jumps while controlling for
MPK, indicating that higher levels of investment are associated with a
greater likelihood of jumps. Conversely, MPK negatively predicts jumps
while controlling for investment, suggesting that lower MPK values are
associated with a higher probability of jumps (Table 1). The baseline
model successfully replicates this pattern.
The core mechanism of the baseline model hinges on this relation-
ship. For a given level of investment across firms, a lower MPK is
associated with a higher probability of a jump. This is because high-
type firms, despite experiencing a low current MPK, are incentivized
to invest due to their higher expected growth prospects. However, this
mechanism is absent in the counterfactual model. Consequently, the
predicted coefficients on investment and MPK in the counterfactual
model are statistically insignificant.
The last two columns of Table 9 present the regression results from
the no-jump model. In the version estimated using Panel A moments of
Table 6, there are no jump realizations. In the model targeting Panel
A and B moments, neither investment nor MPK predict jumps, as their
occurrence is purely driven by random realizations of large Gaussian
shocks.
The cross-sectional dispersion in realized MPK is a commonly used
metric for misallocation in the literature, where firms with low MPK
are deemed to have too much capital relative to their more productive
high MPK counterparts. We next investigate the extent to which cross-
sectional dispersion in MPKs would be reduced in the counterfactual
scenario where firms cease ‘‘investing in misallocation’’ compared to
the baseline model. Fig. 4 illustrates that the interquartile range of
MPK is1 7 . 2 \%lower in the counterfactual model. This reduction in-
dicates a convergence of MPK values among firms. Additionally, the
investment rates of firms also exhibit convergence, leading to a lower
cross-sectional dispersion, particularly among young firms.
The1 7 . 2 \%reduction in overall MPK dispersion may appear modest,
but this figure fails to capture the heterogeneous effects on the cross-
section of firms. A more nuanced understanding of this reduction
emerges when examining MPK dispersion within portfolios categorized
as above- and below-median I/K, as illustrated in Table 10. In the data,

the baseline model generates approximately half of the dispersion

The unique feature of our model implies that it is the MPK dispersion
between diagonal and off-diagonal portfolios sorted on investment and

Similarly, while the no-jump model generates substantial overall MPK
dispersion, as presented in the last two columns of Table 10, it also
completely misses the mark in the contribution of off-diagonal firms to
the MPK dispersion within high and low investment firms.

What does our model reveal about the aggregate impact of eliminat-
ing our mechanism? Specifically, does the reduction in MPK dispersion
in the counterfactual scenario have a positive or negative impact on
overall efficiency? To address this question, we compute the aggregate
Total Factor Productivity (TFP) in both the baseline and counterfactual
models. In particular, we compute the aggregate log TFP in the model
as a Solow residual, obtained by subtracting( 1 - \alpha )times log total
capital from the log total output produced by all firms. Fig. 5 illustrates
a striking4 3 \%reduction in aggregate TFP when firms cease ‘‘investing
in misallocation’’ and the associated reductions in MPK and investment
dispersions take place. While the differences are largely driven by the
investment policies of young firms, which are more likely to be high-
type, Fig. 5 shows that TFP losses in the counterfactual model are more
pronounced for mature firms. This occurs because productivity is path-
dependent, reflecting the cumulative impact of jumps since a firm’s
inception. As a result, mature firms experience greater productivity
losses, having foregone investment in their upside potential during their
early years.
In the baseline model, the infrequent realization of anticipated
jumps leads to substantial increases in productivity when they do occur,
ultimately contributing to an overall rise in TFP. According to our
model, higher investment driven by jump anticipation is justified by
the expected gains from jumps, therefore such investment is expected
to enhance overall productivity in the economy by allocating more
capital to high-type firms. In this sense, our mechanism generates
favorable cross-sectional dispersion in MPK and investment, aligning
with our interpretation of jumps as sources of innovation or other
activities that foster future growth, despite their limited immediate
impact on output. Therefore, a reduction in this beneficial dispersion
implies diminished productivity in the economy, despite being driven
by the avoidance of investments by currently low-productivity firms,
whose investments may superficially resemble misallocation. In Sec-
tion 4.3, we present empirical evidence that supports the notion that
‘‘investments in misallocation’’ have a net positive effect on aggregate
productivity.
It is worth noting that our mechanism is distinct from the conven-
tional view in the literature, which attributes misallocation primarily to
distortions such as adjustment costs or financial constraints (Hsieh and
Klenow, 2009). In this literature, financially constrained firms cannot
invest as much as they efficiently should, leading to persistent under-
investment and high MPK. Removing financial constraints reallocates
capital to high MPK firms, reduces cross-sectional dispersion in MPK,
increases output by directing capital toward more productive firms,
while generally leaving individual firm productivity unchanged (Bau
and Matray, 2023). However, financial constraints — though absent
from our model — are difficult to reconcile with high-investment, low-
MPK firms and would not explain why firms with abundant capital and
low MPK still maintain high investment rates relative to their industry
peers. Similarly, investment frictions generally lead to sluggish capital
adjustment, and eliminating these frictions leads to greater capital
allocation to high MPK firms (David and Venkateswaran, 2019). We
illustrate this point within our model by eliminating capital adjustment
costs in the counterfactual model (Internet Appendix Figure IA.5).
As expected, eliminating adjustment costs significantly reduces MPK

rent productivity. This improvement in allocative efficiency enhances
aggregate efficiency, ultimately increasing TFP in the economy.
Our mechanism, however, challenges the notion that allocating
more resources to low MPK firms is inherently inefficient. Instead,
it highlights that such allocations can be optimal when considering
firms’ future prospects in terms of productivity, innovation, and growth.
Consequently, our results call for caution when interpreting increased
capital allocation to high MPK firms as the only efficient outcome, such
as in cases where financial constraints are lifted. Indeed, our framework
suggests that directing capital toward high-type firms enhances overall
productivity while allowing for the coexistence of low MPK firms. In
summary, the patterns identified in our analysis caution against auto-
matically labeling capital investment in low MPK firms as misallocation
relative to high MPK firms.
4.3. Implications for firm growth and aggregate productivity
The analysis presented in Figs. 4 and 5 highlights the potential
for lower aggregate productivity, despite reduced MPK dispersion, if
firms cease investing in anticipation of jumps. However, our simple
framework assumes independence among firms and thus overlooks po-
tential spillover effects. In reality, firms’ investments and outcomes can
generate both positive and negative spillovers, influencing other firms
in various ways. As a result, the overall impact of our proposed channel
on aggregate outcomes remains uncertain. For instance, investments in
innovation can push the technology frontier, generating benefits for all
firms. Conversely, investments aimed at expanding market share might
adversely affect the growth prospects of other firms, or technologies
developed by one firm could render the technologies of rival firms
obsolete.
To empirically investigate this question, we explore the predictive
relationship between a firm’s future growth and both its own current
investment and that of its competitors. Specifically, we estimate the
following regression model:
\log \frac { Y _ { i , t + 5 } } { Y _ { i , t } } = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \ I / K _ { i , t } + \sum _ { p = 1 , 2 } \sum _ { q = 1 , 2 } \alpha _ { p q } \ I / K _ { c o m p \ \in \ ( I / K _ { p } , M P K _ { q } ) , t } + \eta _ { i } + \epsilon _ { i , t + 5 } ,
where log\log \frac { Y _ { i , t + 5 } } { Y _ { i , t } }represents the 5-year log growth rate in sales, capital,
gross profits, or total factor productivity( \mathrm { T F P } ) ^ { 3 }of\mathrm { f i r m } \ ifrom year ??
\textbf { 0 } t + 5 , \ I / K _ { i , t }denotes the investment rate of the\mathrm { f i r m } \ iin yeart,
I / K _ { c o m p } \in ( I / K _ { p } , M P K _ { q } ) , trefers to the median investment rate of com-
petitor firms operating in the same industry as firm ?? and belonging
to the portfolio( I / K _ { p } , M P K _ { q } ), and\eta _ { i }represents a firm fixed effect,
capturing unobserved heterogeneity specific to each firm. Hence, the
regression model employs time-series variation to estimate the effect
of a firm’s own investment\left( \alpha _ { 1 } \right), as well as its competitors’ investments
— grouped by their positions in the joint distribution of investment and
marginal product of capital( \mathrm { M P K } ) - ( \alpha _ { p q } ), on its future growth. The
specification follows (Kogan et al., 2017), who analyze the role of firms’
own and competitors’ innovation activities in shaping firm growth.
Table 11 reveals significant positive effects of firms’ own invest-
ment on sales growth, capital growth, profit growth, and a higher
ranking in the future total factor productivity (TFP) distribution. A
particularly striking finding is the impact of competitors’ investment.
While investments from other portfolios do not consistently predict
firm growth, a higher median investment by competitor firms in the
( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio predicts lower firm growth and a decline in
future TFP ranking. These findings align with the research conducted
by Kogan et al. (2017) and the empirical findings presented in Sec-
tion 2, which indicate that firms in the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio tend
to engage in a higher degree of innovative activities and make sig-
nificant contributions, as evidenced by patent values and citations.
Interestingly, the use of a broad measure of investment, rather than
precise indicators of innovation, proves sufficient to capture the effect
of competitors on firm growth. This further supports the notion that
firms in the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio hold a distinct role in the economy
compared to other firms. Their observed high investment rates are
motivated by future prospects, encompassing both their own growth
prospects and the potential displacement of competitors.
The negative effect of( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio investment on com-
petitors can be attributed to the dominance of competition and creative
destruction channels, which outweigh the potential benefits of technol-
ogy spillovers, that are not accounted for in our model. Consequently,
the TFP gains derived from our model’s perspective can be considered
an upper bound in quantitative terms. However, a crucial question
remains as to whether the negative effect on competitors is significant
enough to entirely offset the advantages of increased investment stem-
ming from the expectation of substantial improvements in a firm’s own
productivity.
To address this question, we analyze the relationship between firms’
investment rates in portfolios sorted by I/K and MPK and future ag-
gregate TFP growth. We utilize business sector and utilization-adjusted
TFP measures developed by Fernald (2014) and estimate the following
regression model:
\log \frac { T F P _ { t + 5 } } { T F P _ { t } } = \alpha _ { 0 } + \sum _ { p = 1 , 2 } \sum _ { q = 1 , 2 } \alpha _ { p q } ~ I / K _ { ( I / K _ { p } , M P K _ { q } ) } + \sum _ { l = 0 } ^ { 2 } c _ { l } ~ \log T F P _ { t - l } + \epsilon _ { i , t + 5 } .
Here,\log { \frac { T F P _ { t + 5 } } { T F P _ { t } } }represents the 5-year log growth rate in aggre-
gate TFP, andI / \dot { K } _ { ( I / K _ { p } , M P K _ { q } ) }represents the median investment rate
of firms belonging to the( \grave { I } / K _ { p } , M P K _ { q } )portfolio. Additionally, we
incorporate controls for the three lagged values of\log T F P, similar to
the specification used by Kogan et al. (2017), who examine the impact
of economy-wide innovation measures on aggregate growth.
Table 12 presents the results of univariate regressions, showing that
median investment rates in low-investment portfolios( I / K _ { 1 } , M P K _ { 1 } )
and( I / K _ { 1 } , M P K _ { 2 } )do not predict future TFP growth. However, the
investment rates of above-median investment portfolios( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )
and( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 2 } )positively predict future aggregate growth using
both TFP measures. Given the common element in investment rates
across high-investment portfolios, we perform a bivariate predictive
regression using the median investment rates of( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )and
( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 2 } ). The investment rate of the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio
dominates that of( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 2 } )and remains a significant and pos-
itive predictor of aggregate TFP. This finding suggests that the high
investments made by low MPK firms in the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio
contribute to overall productivity growth in the economy, and the
observed effect is economically significant: A one-standard deviation
increase in the median investment rate of firms in the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )
portfolio corresponds to an additional approximately one standard
deviation increase in 5-year TFP growth.
The positive predictability of aggregate TFP by the investments
of the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio indicates that the negative effect on
competitors, as documented in Table 11, is outweighed by the posi-
tive effect on firms’ own growth within the( I / K _ { 2 } , M P K _ { 1 } )portfolio.
In summary, while our previous sections focused on the impact of
high investment–low MPK firms’ investment on their own growth, the
positive relationship between these firms’ investments and aggregate
TFP provides qualitative support for our counterfactual calculation in
Fig. 5, which suggests a reduction in aggregate TFP in the absence of
‘‘investing in misallocation’’.
5. Conclusion
This paper presents a novel empirical finding within Compustat
firms: around2 0 \%of these firms demonstrate above-median invest-
ment rates despite having below-median marginal product of capital.
Within the conventional neoclassical framework, which views differ-
ences in MPK as indicators of misallocation to be avoided, these firms
can be identified as misallocating resources. We argue that such ‘‘in-
vesting in misallocation’’ is not necessarily inefficient and is geared
towards future growth. In particular, these firms possess distinct char-
acteristics, such as being relatively young, and a small proportion
(around4 \%) experience significant jumps in their sales and MPK in
the subsequent year, a rarity among other firms.
To capture the dynamics of heterogeneous firms, we propose and
estimate a simple endogenous firm growth model. This model incorpo-
rates the idea that high-type firms’ investments increase the likelihood
of large upward moves in productivity. It aligns well with the observed
data, explaining the high investment levels exhibited by firms despite
their low MPK. Importantly, this model allows us to conduct counter-
factual analyses by simulating a scenario in which firms are unable to
invest in anticipation of such jumps. In this counterfactual scenario, we
observe a better alignment between investment and MPK, resulting in
a reduction in ‘‘misallocation’’. However, this adjustment also leads to
a decrease in aggregate productivity.
Our contribution deviates from previous literature on misallocation
in several aspects. While prior studies primarily focus on examining
distortions and frictions such as adjustment costs, information asym-
metries, and financial constraints that lead to misallocation, we take
a distinct approach by considering the role of firms’ investments in
shaping their future productivity and show that some of the measured
misallocation may be attributed to an overlooked aspect of optimal
investment policy.
Furthermore, our analysis employs the Compustat sample, which
consists of publicly traded firms with access to public capital markets.
This distinguishes our study from earlier research that often concen-
trates on smaller private firms, frequently from emerging economies,
(e.g., Midrigan and Xu, 2014; Bau and Matray, 2023). By examin-
ing publicly traded firms, which tend to be larger in scale, we mit-
igate the potential impact of financial constraints that can be more
pronounced among smaller private firms. In fact, Farre-Mensa and
Ljungqvist (2016) have documented that publicly traded firms typically
face minimal financial constraints.
In summary, our paper offers a fresh perspective on the interpre-
tation of MPK dispersion as an indicator of misallocation. Through
our empirical analysis and the utilization of the Compustat sample,
we provide new insights into the investment behavior and productivity
dynamics of firms, contributing to a deeper understanding of resource
allocation in the economy.

Can Social Media Inform Corporate Decisions?
Evidence from Merger Withdrawals
J. ANTHONY COOKSON, MARINA NIESSNER,and CHRISTOPH SCHILLER*
ABSTRACT
This paper studies whether social media sentiment predicts merger withdrawals.We
find that a one-standard-deviation increase in social media sentiment after a merger
announcement is associated with a 0.64 percentage point lower probability of with-
drawal (1 6 . 6 \%of the average).This effect is unexplained by abnormal price reactions,
traditional news, and analyst recommendations. Consistent with manager learning,
the informativeness of social media strengthens after firms start corporate Twitter
accounts.The informativeness is driven by longer acquisition-related tweets by fun-
damental investors,rather than memes and price trend tweets. These findings sug-
gest that social media signals can be important for corporate decisions.
THE RAPID EXPANSION OF NEW DATA sources and increasing adoption
of financial technology have transformed the way investors interact with
markets.Today, investors share opinions and investment ideas on social media
platforms (Bradley et al. (2021)), mobile apps allow investors to access finan-
cial information from anywhere via their smartphones (Kalda et al. (2021)),
and sophisticated market participants like hedge funds and analysts rely on
signals from social media (Grennan and Michaely (2021); Dessaint, Foucault,
and Frésard (2024).1 At the same time, investors' use of social platforms was
at the center of recent trading frenzies (Pedersen (2022)), sparking concerns
about how these platforms shape and maintain investor attention (Barber
et al. (2022)). Though scholars debate whether social media benefits investors,
the literature is clear that investor behavior is influenced by social media.
In this paper, we investigate an important related question: does social
media influence the corporate decisions of firm managers? This is a natural
question given that external information, such as prices,investments,and tra-
ditional news,has been shown to shape the decision-making of firm manage-
ment (Edmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2012); Bustamante and Fresard (2021)).
However, given that social media is often a source of noise, it is not obvious
ex ante that social media should be informative for firm managers,unlike, for
example, traditional news.If we find that social media is a useful signal for
firm managers,this would represent novel evidence that social media matters
for corporate information environments, not just investors and markets.
Our analyses show that social media can be valuable for firm decision-
making. We study an important class of corporate investment decisions,
namely, mergers and acquisitions (M&A),which are among the most conse-
quential investments that firms can make (Andrade,Mitchell,and Stafford
2001; Renneboog and Vansteenkiste 2019). Our empirical tests investigate
how the social media reaction to an acquisition's announcement predicts
the decision to withdraw the acquisition. Our core finding is that a negative
social media reaction predicts a significantly higher likelihood that an an-
nounced merger is withdrawn.Our tests control for other drivers of merger
withdrawals, such as market reactions, traditional news coverage,analyst
recommendation changes,and CEO professional networks. Controlling for
these alternative external signals has little effect on the estimated magnitude
of the social media reaction.
Identifying an effect of social media on corporate decisions is empirically
challenging for several reasons. First, reverse causation is possible because
corporate decisions affect social media directly. Indeed,recent work empha-
sizes financial technologies that firms use to communicate with investors
(Blankespoor, Miller, and White 2014; Elliott, Grant, and Hodge 2018). Sec-
ond, frm-specific social media sentiment is difficult to observe across a broad
cross section of firms.Third, even if such information is available, traditional
investment proxies,such as CAPX,are available only quarterly at best, which
does not provide a compelling link to social media information, which arrives
at high frequency.
Our empirical setting overcomes these challenges in two ways. First,we
construct a firm-specific daily measure of social media sentiment using the
investor social platform StockTwits.Like Twitter, StockTwits users share
short messages (henceforth,“tweets") with their followers.However, unlike
Twitter, StockTwits users primarily discuss financial markets and individual
stocks. Users use “cashtags” followed by a ticker (e.g.,\$ 1AAPL for Apple stock)
to tweet about a specific firm.2 Using this information,we measure social
media sentiment for a broad cross section of firms by aggregating tweet-level
sentiment to the stock-day level. Second,we study M&A transactions,which
allow us to observe both the precise merger announcement date and the
ultimate outcome of the transaction (deal completion or withdrawal).Building
on Luo (20o5), the intuition for our main test is simple: as long as announced
acquisitions reflect genuine intentions of firm management to proceed with
the takeover, the decision to withdraw an announced merger reflects an
update in the firm manager's beliefs.If the social media reaction to the merger
announcement predicts merger outcomes holding constant other signals that
influence merger withdrawals,we interpret this as evidence that social media
sentiment is a useful input for corporate decisions.
We implement this empirical strategy using U.S. data on acquisition an-
nouncements of public and private targets from 201O to 2021. We find that a
one-standard-deviation decrease in abnormal StockTwits sentiment predicts
a 0.64 percentage point greater likelihood of merger withdrawal (. 1 6 . 6 \%of the
unconditional likelihood). This estimated coefficient is robust to the inclusion
of other signals (e.g. acquisition announcement cumulative abnormal returns
[CARsl as in Luo (20o5), traditional news sentiment as in Liu and McConnell
(2013),and analyst recommendation changes as in Becher, Cohn,and Juer-
gens (2O15)), deal characteristics,acquirer firm controls,and industry and
year-by-quarter fixed effects.In robustness tests,we control for acquirer CAR
nonlinearly, and we compute CARs using different event windows around
the deal announcement. The estimated magnitudes are similar in these tests,
increasing confidence that the estimated coeffcient is not driven by an omitted
characteristic (e.g., Oster (2019)). Our findings are also robust to social media
sentiment using data from Twitter rather than StockTwits,using alternative
sentiment classifications,focusing on public frms and large firms,controlling
for CEO network size,and examining only serial acquirers while adding firm
fixed effects.
Next, we shed light on the nature of the information contained in the social
media signal via four sets of results.First,we find that the informativeness
of social media sentiment is strongest after firms register a corporate Twitter
account, especially if the account is active,which provides a conduit for firms
to listen to investor commentary from social media. Second, we find that the
informativeness of social media sentiment is driven by the tweets of fun-
damental investors,and is unrelated to the abnormal sentiment emanating
from technical investors who rely primarily on nonfundamental information
such as charts of past volume and returns. Similarly, we find that the in-
formativeness of social media is driven by longer tweets that likely contain
more detailed analysis,not by short tweets that merely convey sentiment.
Third,when we use a language topic model to decompose the social media
signal into tweets that are informative of fundamental information (company
discussions, discussion of deal terms, etc.) versus tweets that are not (meme
tweets, technical tweets),we find that sentiment from fundamental topics
strongly predicts merger withdrawals,whereas sentiment from memes and
technical tweets does not.Fourth,we find that social media sentiment predicts
merger withdrawals most strongly when the associated analyst conference call
uses a high fraction of constraining and negative terms. Consistent with an
external feedback mechanism, this difference emerges from the Q&A portion
of the conference call, not the scripted presentation portion.3
Overall, this evidence helps distinguish between the revelatory nature and
the purely forecasting nature of information on social media. This distinction
draws on similar concepts introduced by Bond, Edmans,and Goldstein (2012)
and Bai, Philippon,and Savov (2O16), who examine revelatory and forecasting
price effciency (FPE). While information on social media undoubtedly has a
forecasting aspect,our evidence-particularly the fact that the predictability
strengthens after a firm establishes a Twitter account—suggests that at least
part of its effect is revelatory to managers.
Although our main findings are consistent with the revelatory channel,
our evidence of learning is indirect.Accordingly, we empirically evaluate two
alternative interpretations of the main result.First,social media may not
lead managers to update their evaluation of the merger, but it could act as
an external governance mechanism as in Ang et al. (2021),who study the
impact of comments on a Chinese message boards on merger withdrawals
in a sample of 303 value-destroying mergers. Under this alternative view,
empire-building managers learn from social media whether their actions
are being monitored,and thus whether they will be held to account by in-
vestors.Under a governance mechanism,a negative social media reaction
is informative to managers because it checks their ability to extract private
benefits.Two results in our setting distinguish our findings from a governance
mechanism.First,a governance channel predicts that the coefficient on social
media sentiment is larger for negative-CAR mergers than for positive-CAR
mergers,but we estimate similar coeffcients across both subsamples.Second,
we estimate similar coefficients on social media sentiment for acquirers with
above-median versus below-median governance metrics,even among deals
with negative CARs. This evidence suggests that our estimates are not driven
primarily by a governance mechanism.
Another alternative interpretation is that social media is informative about
the final outcome of the deal but only because it correlates with unmea-
sured factors that predict merger withdrawals. To evaluate this channel, we
estimate how social media sentiment predicts merger withdrawals for deals
blocked by regulators and deals rejected by the target,which cannot be driven
by manager learning. Though statistically significant, the estimated mag-
nitude on this nonlearning subsample is only one-third of the full-sample
result, consistent with the main result reflecting learning. As further evi-
dence of a learning channel, we find that social media's effect on merger
withdrawals strengthens after the firm adopts a Twitter account. Together
with controlling for alternative learning channels (e.g., CEO network size),
this evidence is consistent with the social media effect driven by manager
learning.
Our paper makes several contributions to the growing literature on how
FinTech influences firms and markets (Philippon 2016;2019). Our analysis
provides a novel perspective on the informativeness of FinTech by showing
that social media sentiment is a valuable signal of the likelihood of merger
withdrawal.The literature is divided on whether the introduction of FinTech
that increases access to data, like social media platforms,increases infor-
mativeness. On the one hand, social media can amplify behavioral biases
(Heimer 2O16),reflect inefficient patterns of attention (Barber et al. 2022;
Cookson, Engelberg,and Mullins 2O22), lead to trading frenzies (Pedersen
2022),and induce inefficient information processing (Bradley et al. 2021).4 On
the other hand, firms devote signifcant resources to social media engagement
(Blankespoor, Miller, and White 2014),and subscribe to services that provide
social media analytics,and in normal times, social media signals contain valu-
able information content (Bartov, Faurel, and Mohanram 2O18; Farrell et al.
2022). Our results contribute to this literature in two ways. First,our results
support the view that social media signals can be informative. Second, unlike
most of this literature,which studies social media impacts on investors and
financial market responses, we show that social media can be informative for
firms.
Our findings are also relevant to the literature at the intersection of finan-
cial markets and corporate decisions (e.g., Chen, Goldstein, and Jiang 2006,
Foucault and Frésard 2012;2014,Edmans,Jayaraman,and Schneemeier
2017,Bond,Edmans,and Goldstein 2012,and Goldstein 2023),a topic that
has been of interest for decades (Morck et al.199O). This literature shows
that stock market reactions help inform corporate decisions across a variety of
contexts, including M&A, seasoned equity offerings (SEOs),and management
earnings forecasts (Luo 2005; Kau, Linck,and Rubin 2008; Giammarino et al.
2004; Zuo 2016). Beyond these well-documented price feedback effects in the
literature,our results show that nonprice signals from social media are an
important part of the firm's information environment.In this respect,our
findings closely relate to Liu and McConnell (2013),who study the informa-
tiveness of traditional media sentiment for M&As. Our setting is also similar
to Ang et al. (2021),who study the responsiveness of merger withdrawals to
negative commentary from a Chinese message board.In contrast to this work,
which emphasizes a governance mechanism, our evidence from a comprehen-
sive U.S. sample shows that social media can provide an evaluative role that
affects deal completion,even for value-creating mergers.5 Overall, our findings
imply that social media is an important and distinct nontraditional source of
information that has similar weight in management decision-making as both
market signals and traditional media.
Our paper also contributes to the literature on FinTech and firms' decision-
making.Previous research examines the uptake of robo-advising, stressing
its challenges as a new technology (D'Acunto, Prabhala,and Rossi 2019).
Recent work also examines the implications of mobile apps and other financial
platforms, emphasizing their uptake by consumers (Olafsson and Pagel 2018;
Benartzi and Levi 202O). This research implies tangible benefits to firms, par-
ticularly financial firms that adopt FinTech to serve their customers.Relative
to this literature,our research shows an impact of FinTech beyond the finan-
cial firms that employ it as a business strategy. In this respect, our paper is
closely related to research that examines the use of social media as a channel
for strategic information disclosure and investor relations management (e.g.,
Jung et al. 2017, Blankespoor, Miller, and White 2014,and Elliott,Grant, and
Hodge 2018).We complement this literature by showing that social media is
not merely a tool for disclosure,but can also be a source of information that
can shape firms' investment decisions.
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.In Section I, we describe
the data and present descriptive statistics. In Section I, we present our main
results and present key robustness tests. In Section III, we present tests of
heterogeneity and mechanisms related to the informativeness of social media
for firm managers. In Section IV, we conclude.
I. Data and Descriptive Statistics
This section describes the paper's data sources,outlines the construction
of our main variables of interest,and reports summary statistics for the key
dependent and independent variables.
A. Financial Social Media
We measure social media sentiment using the financial social media network
StockTwits.Like Twitter, StockTwits users post tweets with a limited num-
ber of characters. However, unlike Twitter, StockTwits focuses primarily on
financial markets.By including a“cashtag,”a dollar sign followed by a ticker
symbol, users specify that their post refers to a specific firm or security. For
example, a StockTwits user could express a positive opinion about Apple Inc.
by saying{ \mathfrak { s o a s o } } \mathbf { L }is making a great acquisition, you should buy!” Cash-tagged
tweets unambiguously identify which companies are discussed for a large sam-
ple of tweets.In addition, StockTwits allows users to flag whether their tweet
reflects “bullish”or“bearish”sentiment.
Launched in 20o8, the StockTwits platform is one of the most established
financial social media platforms.In 2020,users generated over 6.5 million
tweets per month. The StockTwits newsfeed is integrated into many online
platforms used by finance professionals,including S&P Capital IQ, Yahoo!
Finance,and Reuters, which allows users to share their ideas directly without
having to log onto StockTwits' website or app. StockTwits has become pop-
ular among both financial market participants and professionals, including
asset and investment managers, news letter writers, and financial journalists
(Cookson and Niessner 2020).
Our data include 260 million tweets posted to StockTwits between January
2010 and December 2021.For each tweet, we observe the timestamp,raw text,
and user-provided sentiment tags.We retain tweets that include at most two
cashtags about any publicly listed U.S.company. Most tweets in our sample
( 9 0 . 3 3 \% )include exactly one cashtag. This restriction unambiguously links a
tweet to a specific stock by dropping tweets when users include multiple pop-
ular cashtags (e.g.,\$ 5 B,\$ 6000,\$ 1AAPL) to generate attention or share their
opinion on a sector or industry.
StockTwits also provides a sentiment score for each tweet,which is calcu-
lated using a proprietary text classification algorithm called MarketLex. This
methodology uses lexical and semantic rules based on a custom-built lexicon
for social finance,constructed from a combination of words and phrases from
4 million messages with user-provided bullish or bearish tags and manual hu-
man supervision.The final sentiment score for each tweet is a continuous mea-
sure that is normalized to be between^ { - 1 }(very bearish) and+ 1(very bullish).
To illustrate the type of information posted to StockTwits,Figure 1 presents
several tweets about Adobe's recent announcement to acquire Figma,a com-
pany that developed a web application for interface design. Panel A shows
that some users post fundamental information in support of their views,
Panel A: Fundamental Tweets about Merger
ChartMill Sep16,2022 12:26 PM
$ADBE shows a strong growth in EPS:1 2 . 8 1 \%, which is quite good.
chartmill.com/stock/quote./...
InvestorsAnteDOTcom Sep 16,2022 11:59 AM
$ADBE will now transform their technology (into cloud & collab) with the code from
FIGMA (no need to hire additional engineers to R&D for years)..and then jack up the
prices (seems like the expectation is for prices to go up nearly30 \%
Market share owned by Adobe after acquisition - roughly9 0 \%(TAM I'd estimated to
be over 16 billion)
Plus
$ADBE NEW ARTICLE: Adobe Is Having a Very Bad Week as Stock Gets Downgraded
on Figma Deal stck.pro/news/ADBE
while Panel B shows that other users describe technical strategies. Thus,
within the StockTwits platform,different kinds of messages should provide
different amounts of information to managers. In the mechanisms section,
we exploit these differences to shed light onto what information matters for
firm managers.
To provide further confidence in our analysis,we separately calculate our
own sentiment scores based on the content of each tweet, trained on bullish
and bearish tags. Specifically, we apply the maximum entropy and naive
Bayes classifier algorithms to the raw text content to classify tweet senti-
ment, following Antweiler and Frank (2004), Cookson and Niessner (2020),
and others. Section I in the Internet Appendix describes the procedure and
performs a validation test confirming that our text classification approach
reliably classifies tweet sentiment.6
As a robustness check,we also include social media sentiment from Twitter,
which is provided by the social media data provider Social Market Analyt-
ics (SMA). SMA uses natural language processing, topic modeling, and source
rating to identify posts related to firms and financial content,and constructs
measures of social media sentiment, post volume,and social media “buzz" from
individual messages posted to Twitter. SMA aggregates Twitter information
for each stock in their coverage at the daily frequency, starting in 2012.
B. Mergers & Acquisitions
Our sample of M&A deals comes from SDC Platinum.We obtain all mergers
announced between 2010 and 2021 with a minimum deal value of\$ 25million.
To match with StockTwits sentiment,we focus on deals with a publicly listed
(U.S.)acquirer. This filter yields 7,726 unique deals. SDC Platinum provides
the announcement date,deal value,and percentage of shares sought.In
addition,we classify whether each announced deal was ultimately completed
or withdrawn,and if withdrawn,we record the withdrawal date. We drop
pending and intended deals,so all deals in our sample are either completed
or withdrawn.
Following the literature (e.g., Bates and Lemmon 2003; Luo 2005; Boone and
Mulherin 2007; Betton et al. 2014; Jacobsen 2014), we obtain data on deal char-
acteristics that relate to merger withdrawals, such as the type of deal payment
(cash vs. stock), the presence of a white knight,antitakeover provisions, and
the presence of rumors prior to deal announcement as controls in the sample.
A detailed description of all deal characteristics obtained from SDC Platinum
is available in the Appendix.
In addition,we compute CARs for both the acquiring and target firms in
event windows around each M&A announcement, using the Fama-French
Three-Factor model and stock return data from the Center for Research in
Security Prices (CRSP).We use an estimation period of 100 days with a mini-
mum of 70 observations and a gap of10 days between the end of the estimation
period and the event period to compute expected and abnormal returns.For
private targets,stock return data are unavailable,which reduces our sample
when we include target-firm CARs.We obtain standard data from Compustat
North America.Each variable (market capitalization,cash holdings,and lever-
age) is winsorized at the5 \%level within the full Compustat universe.We also
obtain data on analyst recommendations from IBES and construct changes in
analyst recommendations following Becher, Cohn, and Juergens (2015).
C. Traditional News Media
To control for traditional news, we collect data from RavenPack.RavenPack
(v.RPA 1.0) provides coverage of premium newswires (such as Dow Jones, Ben-
zinga, Alliance News, and FX Street), many providers of regulatory news and
press releases,and over 22,0oO web publications. Specifically, we obtain the
Event Sentiment Score (ESS) of all news articles and reports published during
the[ - 1 ; + 2 ]window around the M&A announcement relevant to the acquirer
and target firms,excluding reposted and older stories.To focus on news perti-
nent to the M&A announcement, we retain only articles and stories related to
“mergers/acquisitions” as categorized by RavenPack.7
D. Analyst Conference Call Transcripts
We obtain analyst conference call transcripts from Refinitiv's Transcripts
and Briefs data set, formerly known as StateStreet.We focus on the transcripts
that are tagged as “M&A Calls / Presentations.”We separately identify the
section of the analyst conference call transcripts related to the management's
scripted presentation and the analyst Q&A section to distinguish content from
the manager and content external to the firm. Specifically, we first calculate the
proportion of“constrained,”“negative,”and “positive”words in the presenta-
tion and Q&A sections of the transcript (Bodnaruk,Loughran, and McDonald
2015; Loughran and McDonald 2016) using the 2022 version of the Loughran
and McDonald (2O11) Master Dictionary. We then calculate for each transcript
the average word length, number of words,and number of unique words of the
two sections as control variables.
E. Tweets about M&A Deals
We first verify that users discuss M&A deals in their tweets. Figure 2 plots
the daily number of tweets about the acquirer and target firms in an event
window. For both targets and acquirers, the number of tweets is stable before
the M&A announcement day and increases sharply on dayst = 0andt = 1,
indicating that the merger announcement is not anticipated by social media
users and creates a significant increase in social media activity.
Figure 3 confirms that this increase in tweets is related to merger announce-
ments by showing the time pattern of the number of tweets that comention
the target's cashtag alongside the acquirer around the merger announcement.
Before the merger announcement, the average number of comention tweets
is indistinguishable from zero. After the merger announcement, this number
increases from zero to approximately 2O,similar to the increase in the total
number of tweets documented in Figure 2.
Figure 4 shows how the M&A content of tweets shifts after the merger
announcement. For both the acquirer (Panel A) and the target (Panel),we
plot the proportion of tweets that mention M&A related words (i.e.,“merger,”
“acquisition,”“m&a,”“takeover,”“acquirer,” and“target").Figure 4 shows that
the proportion of tweets containing these M&A words more than quadru-
ples on the day of the merger announcement and stays elevated for at least
10 days.
F. Variable Construction and Summary Statistics
Table I presents summary statistics for key variables, split by deals that
were completed versus withdrawn. Internet Appendix Table IA.II presents
summary statistics for the full set of variables in the paper. The final sam-
ple of M&A announcements with available StockTwits sentiment data for the
acquiring firms contains 6,438 unique M&A deals,out of which 6,187 were
eventually completed and 251 withdrawn.We thus obtain a deal withdrawal
rate of approximately3 . 9 \%,consistent prior literature (e.g.,Luo (2005)).Most
M&A transactions in the sample are full takeovers, with the median percent-
age of shares sought equal to100 \%(mean of9 6 . 7 1 \%for completed deals and
9 5 . 9 1 \%for withdrawn deals).
The differences between completed and withdrawn samples in Panel A are
consistent with prior work on M&A completion.For example,acquirer CARs
and traditional news sentiment are greater for completed deals than for with-
drawn deals as in Luo (2005), Liu and McConnell(2013), and Ang et al. (2021).
The social media signal contains significant information around announce-
ments of both completed and withdrawn mergers.As seen in Panel B, the aver-
age number of tweets about the acquiring firm around the M&A announcement
date is 193.32 for completed mergers,and it is 148.04 for withdrawn mergers.
This difference,however, is not statistically significant.
We seek to evaluate whether differences in social media sentiment are
informative about deal completion. To account for the fact that StockTwits
sentiment varies significantly across stocks, we calculate abnormal sentiment
for each M&A announcement:

II. Results
A. Information from Social Media and M&A Withdrawals
In this section, we evaluate whether social media contains unique informa-
tion that is useful for predicting the likelihood of merger withdrawal beyond
other public signals that firm managers are known to rely on (e.g., the market
reaction to the merger announcement, traditional news media,and analyst
recommendations (Luo 2005; Liu and McConnell 2013; Ang et al. 2021)) and
we seek to quantify its importance relative to these other signals.
Using deal-level information,we estimate the linear probability model9

The estimated coefficient onC A R [ - 1 , 1 0 ]highlights the importance of mar-

the acquiring firm around the announcement, which reduces the concern that
attention to the deal and not sentiment drives the main result.

around the announcement of the merger. Following Becher, Cohn,and Juer-

nus the sum of analyst downgrades and coverage initiations with a “(strong)

B. Evaluating Social Media as a Governance Mechanism
A plausible economic channel behind the results so far is that managers pur-
sue private interests through their M&A decisions (e.g.,empire building as in
Moeller, Schlingemann,and Stulz 2005). In this case, CEOs might knowingly
announce value-destroying acquisitions,but the negative feedback from social
media might discipline their behavior by informing the board or other stake-
holders,similar to the role of traditional news media (e.g., Liu and McConnell
2013). Consistent with this governance mechanism,Ang et al. (2021) find that
negative feedback on a Chinese message board leads to the withdrawal of
value-destroying mergers. Ang et al. (2021) focus explicitly on negative-CAR
merger announcements because this is where external governance should be
effective. The information revelation mechanism that we emphasize, however,
should be relevant for both positive- and negative-CAR mergers.
To explore the importance of this governance mechanism in our sample, we
split our sample into M&A deals with positive and negative announcement
returns. If our results were driven purely by a governance mechanism as in
Ang et al. (2021),AbnSent should not predict merger withdrawals for positive-
CAR deals. However, as we see in Figure 5, the estimated coeffcient is similar
whether the announcement return is positive or negative.14
Next, we construct measures of internal and external corporate governance
following prior literature,and focus on the subsample of deals with negative
announcement CARs as in Ang et al. (2021). Under the governance inter-
pretation,AbnSent should exhibit a stronger relation to merger withdrawals
when the firm's corporate governance is weaker, as managers in firms with
strong corporate governance are ex ante less likely to knowingly engage in
value-destroying acquisitions. Specifically, we construct measures of board
independence using data from the Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS)
following Guo and Masulis (2015), Coles,Daniel, and Naveen (2014),and
Ferreira, Ferreira,and Mariano (2018), board monitoring intensity following
Fich and Shivdasani (2006), Faleye, Hoitash,and Hoitash (2011),and Field,
Lowry, and Mkrtchyan (2013),and antitakeover provisions following Gompers,
Ishii,and Metrick (2003) and Bebchuk, Cohen,and Ferrell (2009).
Table IA.VI presents these sample split results. Broadly speaking,we
estimate an inconsistent and insignificant pattern regarding the estimated
coeffcient on AbnSent in weaker- versus stronger-governance firms. For
example, splitting on high versus low numbers of independent directors and
non-co-opted directors (Coles,Daniel,and Naveen 2O14),as well as CEOs
with a dual role in Panel A,we find a negative coefficient on AbnSent that is
statistically indistinguishable across high- versus low-governance firms. Sim-
ilarly, we estimate that the coefficient on AbnSent is similar when we split the
sample by whether acquirer firms have more distracted or busy boards of direc-
tors (Panel B) or whether firms have more antitakeover provisions (Panel C).
Taken together, these findings suggest that social media as a governance mech-
anism alone cannot explain the main finding in our sample of U.S.M&A deals.
In Section III, we present a battery of tests that support our interpretation
that social media is informative, revealing relevant information to managers.
C. Robustness
In this section, we present several robustness exercises and subsample tests
that highlight the pervasiveness of the relationship between social media sen-
timent and merger withdrawals.
C.1. Alternative Sentiment Measures
A natural question that arises is whether the abnormal sentiment from
StockTwits is unique,or whether sentiment from other social media sites (e.g.,
Twitter) would paint the same picture. To address this question,we obtain
firm-day Twitter sentiment scores from SMA,a firm that provides sentiment
information to professional investors.We construct a similar abnormal Twitter
sentiment measure around the merger announcements from SMA's sentiment
data for our sample time period. SMA data are only available starting from
2012.Thus,we are only able to construct this measure on the subset of
merger announcements from 2012 onward.However, as we show in column
(1) of Table II,Panel B, the coeffcient estimate on the StockTwits abnormal
sentiment measure\left( - 0 . 7 0 6 \right)is similar to the full-sample estimate.
Next,column (2) presents the result from estimating the specification
using abnormal Twitter sentiment.We estimate a strong negative relation
between abnormal Twitter sentiment and merger withdrawal likelihood:a
one-standard-deviation decrease in Twitter sentiment is associated with a
1.075 percentage point greater likelihood of merger withdrawal. This finding
suggests that social media is more broadly related to merger withdrawal.
Finally, column (3)presents estimates from a specification that includes both
abnormal sentiment measures (StockTwits and Twitter). Strikingly, both sig-
nals remain highly statistically significant and negatively related to merger
withdrawals even after holding constant the other abnormal sentiment mea-
sure.The fact that both signals have distinctive content suggests that dif-
ferent social media signals contain different information regarding the likeli-
hood of merger withdrawal.This finding makes sense given that the sentiment
signals from StockTwits and Twitter are distinct from one another (Cookson
et al. 2024). For example, StockTwits users focus specifically on investment
topics,whereas Twitter investors might discuss a wider array of topics related
to firms.
Another issue with employing sentiment measures is that measuring social
media sentiment is inherently noisy. To alleviate concerns that these black-box
measures were constructed in a way that leads to the correlation with merger
withdrawals, we next examine whether the main results are robust to alterna-
tive measures of social media sentiment that we construct ourselves. Specifi-
cally, we construct textual classifications by training a maximum entropy clas-
sifier and a Bayesian classifier, and we use each of these classifiers to impute
the sentiment of unclassified tweets.15 We then aggregate the sentiment from
these alternative classifications to the stock-day level and use this modified
sentiment index to construct measures of abnormal social media sentiment,
following the same procedure as for the main measure detailed in Section I.F.
Columns (1) and (2) Table III, Panel A present the estimates using these
alternative measures of social media M&A announcement reaction.A one-
standard-deviation increase in these alternative measures is associated with a
0.427 to 0.584 percentage point lower likelihood of merger withdrawal, which
is somewhat smaller than the magnitudes we obtain using StockTwits’ pri-
mary sentiment measure. The qualitative conclusion, however is the same: a
more negative AbnSent is associated with a greater likelihood of merger with-
drawal. It makes sense that the signal using StockTwits' algorithm or SMA's
algorithm has more predictive power. Managers might buy social media sig-
nals about their own firm from StockTwits directly or from vendors like SMA,
in which case they would be learning from those signals, rather than our self-
classified versions.For the rest of the paper, therefore,we use the sentiment
measure provided by StockTwits.
C.2. Public Targets and Target Sentiment
Public targets represent a minority of the mergers in our sample-only 880
mergers with public targets out of 6,306 in the full sample-but these merg-
ers account for most of the aggregate deal value and they attract both signif-
icant scrutiny from investors and outsized media coverage (both traditional
and social). In column (3) of Table II, Panel A, we present estimates using
the subsample of deals with public targets.We also control for the market re-
action for the target stock,using both targetC A R [ - 5 , - 1 ]andC A R [ - 1,10].
We obtain an estimated magnitude that is much larger than our main esti-
mates in Table II: a one-standard-deviation decrease in abnormal sentiment
is associated with a 2.13 percentage point increase in the likelihood of merger
withdrawal. This represents approximately53 \%of the baseline likelihood of
merger withdrawals for the sample of public merger announcements.
For deals with public targets, we also construct an abnormal sentiment mea-
sure for the target firm. The correlation between the abnormal sentiment
about the acquirer and the target is O.9.However, despite this high correla-
tion,controlling for the abnormal sentiment about the target—column (4) of
Table III, Panel A-does not meaningfully change the estimated impact of ac-
quirer abnormal sentiment,and the target's abnormal sentiment does not ex-
plain merger withdrawals beyond the acquirer's abnormal sentiment. Column
(5) additionally controls for the target's market reaction to the deal announce-
ment,which does not meaningfully influence the estimate on the abnormal
sentiment term.
In column (6),we additionally include the target spread-that is, the per-
centage difference between the offer price and the target stock price two days
after the merger announcement—as a market-based proxy for the likelihood
of deal completion.In line with the idea that a larger spread between offer
price and target stock price indicates a higher probability that the deal falls
through,we estimate a significant positive coefficient on the target spread.
However, the inclusion of the target spread term does not materially affect our
estimated coefficient on AbnSenti. Thus the social media term does not merely
reflect the market evaluation of the likelihood of the deal going through,but
rather contains unique information about the likelihood of deal completion.
C.3. Small Deals and Serial Acquirers
Next,we reestimate the main specification after dropping small deals (i.e.,
those in the bottom quartile of deal value).In column(7),we see that in the
remaining large-firm subsample,there is a slightly larger sensitivity of merger
withdrawals to social media sentiment: a one-standard-deviation increase in
abnormal social media sentiment is associated with a 1.171 percentage point
decline in the likelihood of a merger withdrawal.
Finally, we address the concern that acquirer-specifc unobservable charac-
teristics drive the relation between social media sentiment and merger with-
drawal. To do so,in column (8) we estimate equation (2) with acquirer fixed ef-
fects as in Golubov, Yawson,and Zhang (2015).This specification relies purely
on within-acquirer variation in merger withdrawals and social media senti-
ment,and therefore can only be estimated for the subset of acquirers that
make at least two acquisition announcements in our sample.Despite the strin-
gency of this test, the relationship between social media sentiment and merger
withdrawal stays similar after accounting for acquirer fixed effects.
C.4. Reasons for Merger Withdrawal
Some merger withdrawal decisions are made by entities outside of the ac-
quiring firm (e.g.,regulators or target firms),and therefore cannot reflect
learning by acquiring firm managers from the information in social media. In
this case, social media is useful to predict eventual merger withdrawal, but the
merger cannot be withdrawn on account of feedback to the manager. To under-
stand the extent to which these nonfeedback reasons for withdrawal contribute
to our results,we use the “deal history” field in SDC Platinum to restrict our
sample to mergers withdrawn for regulatory reasons and mergers withdrawn
by the target shareholders or board.
Panel B of Table II presents the estimated coefficients after restricting our
sample to deals that were withdrawn for external reasons. The estimated coef-
ficient is- 0 . 2 0 2,for regulator-withdrawn mergers,while for target-withdrawn
mergers it is- 0 . 1 5 0. While the AbnSent coeffcient estimate is statistically
significant at the5 \%level for regulator-withdrawn mergers, the magnitude is
less than one-third of the magnitude in our main tests (and is insignificant
for target-withdrawn mergers). Thus,much of the relationship of social me-
dia sentiment and merger withdrawal likelihood goes away when we focus on
cases in which social media plays purely a predictive role.
D. Social Media Sentiment and Eventual Market Returns
A natural question that arises is whether abnormal social media sentiment
is a signal that improves or worsens the acquirer firm's outcomes. To pro-
vide partial evidence on this question,we estimate how the market eventu-
ally responds to news of merger withdrawal. Here, we focus on the entire win-
dow from deal announcement to conclusion because information about even-
tual withdrawal often occurs well before the deal's official conclusion.Accord-
ingly, we take the buy-and-hold abnormal return over the entire interim period
( B H A R _ { [ 1 1 ; T _ { c o n c l u s i o n } ] } )to be the dependent variable in a regression that relates
AbnSent to the subsequent stock returns,interacted with the deal outcome.
Specifically, we estimate

III. Heterogeneity and Mechanisms
In this section,we present several tests that provide sharper insight into
the nature of the information contained in abnormal social media sentiment,
as well as how it is potentially used by managers to inform their merger
withdrawal decisions.Broadly, the tests in this section aim to distinguish
between whether social media is informative in a revelatory sense versus a
pure forecasting sense.This distinction is guided by a similar distinction made
by Bond, Edmans,and Goldstein (2012) and Bai, Philippon,and Savov (2016)
about revelatory price efficiency (RPE)or FPE.Although social media un-
doubtedly contains information that would already be available to managers
(akin to the FPE interpretation),the tests in this section suggest that social
media information is also revelatory to the manager.16
A. Acquirers' Social Media Engagement
Our main tests draw a robust connection between abnormal social media
sentiment and the likelihood of merger withdrawal. The social media senti-
ment reaction could be informative of merger withdrawal decisions because it
correlates with another information source that managers use (i.e., it is useful
in forecasting),or because social media is a source of information unto itself
(i.e.,it is revelatory to the manager).To shed light on the relative weight of
these two channels, we test whether the relation between merger withdrawals
and social media sentiment strengthens when managers are more likely to
be listening to social media. To do so, we assemble a comprehensive data set
of corporate Twitter accounts for the acquirer firms in our sample.From the
profle information,we observe the account registration date, the number of
followers,and whether the account is a verified account.17
If firms use social media as an information source, the estimated coefficient
on AbnSent should be larger for acquisitions in which the acquirer is engaged
with social media, either because those firms are more likely to see posts about
their frms,or because the managers are more likely to be open to purchasing
social media signals from vendors.Indeed, this is what we find: in the Internet
Appendix, Table IA.VII presents the results from a sample split, which high-
lights that acquirers that have a Twitter presence at the time of the merger
announcement exhibit a much stronger link between AbnSent and the likeli-
hood of merger withdrawal.
To formalize this relationship,we construct a test that uses the timing when
the corporate Twitter account was registered. Specifically, we estimate

B. Nature of Social Media Content
If acquirers use social media to inform corporate decisions, a natural ques-
tion that arises is whether acquirers are listening to noise or informed analysis.
If the sensitivity to AbnSent is driven by more informed content, this would
reinforce the revelatory information interpretation.In this vein,we present
four complementary tests to understand the information content of the social
media signal. First, we employ a prominent feature of StockTwits,namely, that
many of the investors are technical traders who trade on price patterns rather
than fundamental information. Second,we split the social media signal into
the signals from short messages versus longer messages,as the latter are more
likely to contain in-depth analysis.Third,we focus on tweets that talk about
M&A-related topics,as determined by a Biterm Topic Model (BTM). Finally,
we split tweets into before and after the GameStop episode of January 2021.
Many users on StockTwits select an investment philosophy in their profile.
For users who do not, we employ a textual classifier to label tweets as technical
or not (a category we call“fundamental").19 Consistent with technical investors
mostly ignoring fundamental information, Figure 6,Panel A shows that the
number of tweets by technical investors does not spike around the merger an-
nouncement,whereas the number of tweets by fundamental investors increase
significantly. More strikingly, Panel B shows that the number of tweets that
discuss M&A-related content or mention the target firm remains flat for tech-
nical investors while it spikes significantly for fundamental StockTwits users
around the merger announcement.
We use this split of users to construct separate AbnSent signals for techni-
cal investors and fundamental investors. To relate this information to merger
withdrawal likelihood,we include both signals in a specification like our base-
line specification in equation (2).
Table VI, Panel A presents the results from this exercise. Consistent with
managers reacting to fundamental information,we find that AbnSent from
the fundamental users exhibits a strong negative relation to merger with-
drawals,whereas the signal from technical investors does not. Specifically, a
one-standard-deviation increase in fundamental sentiment is associated with
a 1.08 percentage point reduction in merger withdrawal likelihood. The anal-
ogous coefficient on AbnSent from technical investors is only- 0 . 2 7 4and is
statistically insignificant.
to be more informative of their merger withdrawal decisions.However,this interpretation requires
that managers voluntarily reveal to investors through social media negative information relevant
to the merger they just announced.This mechanism therefore seems less plausible.Another pos-
sibility is that managers listen more intently to their other information sources after their firm
createsa Twitter account.To evaluate this possibility,in Table IA.IX, we repeat the analysis while
controlling for proxies for the size of the manager's professional network as wellas the interactions
between these proxies and the acquirer CAR.Consistent with a revelatory information interpre-
tation ofthe findings in this section,we obtain similar estimates on the interaction between active
Twitter accounts and the social media signal.

We complement the technical versus fundamental split by investigating
whether the social media signal is stronger if it comes from longer messages.
Long tweets tend to contain more in-depth analysis than short tweets (e.g.,
see the examples in Figure 1).As with the technical versus fundamental split,
we construct separate AbnSent signals based on the subset of messages with
below-median tweet length and messages with above-median tweet length,
and we include both long- and short-tweet signals in the same specification.
As we see in columns (3) and (4) of Table VI, Panel A, the merger withdrawal
likelihood bears a strong negative relation to the long-tweet abnormal senti-
ment while bearing no relation to shorter tweets.
Next,we perform a contextual analysis of the tweet topics,and we relate
different-context tweets to merger withdrawals. To classify tweets into dif-
ferent topics,we train a BTM to the StockTwits tweet corpus.BTM tends
to outperform alternative topic models (e.g.,Latent Dirichlet Allocation)
when the corpus is populated by many short texts,such as tweets (Yan
et al. 2013).20 We estimate the BTM with eight topics on the sample of all
tweets from the [0,3] window around merger announcements. Our focus is
on six of the identified topics that contain meaningful content relevant to
the merger announcement, which we label “Company/Business,”“Disclosure,”
“Deal Terms,”“Trading,”“Technical,” and “Memes.” These topics highlight
conceptually distinct discussions on StockTwits.For instance, the Trading
topic is dominated by discussions of specific positions (buy, sell), the Disclosure
topic includes discussions of M&A-specific filings (file, report),the Meme topic
most commonly features emojis (face,rocket),and the Technical topic features
discussions of technical indicators (price, volume, and chart). Figure IA.4
reports the top 2O words in each topic.
After classifying each tweet into its topic,we construct separate abnormal
sentiment signals by topic for each deal, and we relate these measures to
merger withdrawals as in our main specification. The most populated topic
is the“Trading” topic,which comprises4 2 . 4 \%of tweets,and thus all specif-
cations include the abnormal sentiment term based on“Trading"” tweets.We
then separately include the other topics’ sentiment terms. Table VI, Panel B
presents the results from estimating these specifications. Consistent with the
abnormal sentiment from M&A-relevant discussions driving the result, we
estimate a significant negative coefficient on AbnSent for each M&A-relevant
topic (i.e.,“M&A Business/Company,”“Disclosure,”and“Deal Terms").How-
ever,we see little relation to merger withdrawals related to the baseline
“Trading” term, nor any impact on sentiment drawn from“Meme”tweets or
“Technical" tweets. These findings reinforce our main interpretation that the
sentiment effect is driven by fundamentally oriented discussions on social
media. The contextual relevance of these discussions increases the plausibility
that social media is revelatory to the manager.
As a complement to these cross-sectional tests,we also perform a test
that relies on a sharp change in the informativeness of investor social media
sentiment-the rise of meme stock conversations after the GameStop (GME)
short squeeze. Bradley et al.(2021) show that after the GME event in January
2021,Reddit became less informative as a predictor of future returns, while
Cookson et al. (2024) show that StockTwits, Twitter, and Seeking Alpha saw a
similar decline in informativeness.Based on this logic,we consider the inter-
action between AbnSent and a post-GME indicator in Table IA.X. Consistent
with meme discussions ofering less information,we find that after January
2021,social media sentiment becomes less informative for whether a merger
is withdrawn.Furthermore,we do not see a similar decline in informativeness
of market signals (i.e., we estimate an insignificant interaction between post-
GME and CAR),which uniquely pins our evidence to the quality of the social
media signal. Taken together, the evidence in this section suggests that so-
cial media is informative insofar as it contains in-depth analysis beyond pure
expressions of sentiment.
